Month in which the riot took place; the affair inspired International Workers' Day, on the first day of this month.
Specific labor policy that workers agitated for via a general strike leading up to the riot.
questions.answer: eight-hour work day (prompt on partial answers like "working limits" or "working conditions")

questions.clue: Labor group, led by Terence Powderly, whose membership plummeted after the riot.
questions.answer: Noble and Holy Order of the Knights of Labor

questions.clue: Activist and publisher of the Arbeiter-Zeitung who spoke at the square and was executed for accessory to murder.
Farm machinery company whose plant was the site of protests the day before the riot.
Governor who pardoned three of the condemned and was voted out of office 4 years later.
Anarchist whose apartment was searched for bomb-making materials; he committed suicide before his execution.
Country where she ruled as Empress for over three decades.
Husband of Catherine who was overthrown in 1762.
Gemstone, named for Orlov, placed in Catherine's scepter. A blue one of these is named for the Hope family.
Two-word term for absolute monarchs like Catherine who supported education, healthcare, and other progressive ideals.
Lover of Catherine who helped negotiate the end of a war against Turkey and names a famous battleship.
Lover of Catherine, who helped him become king of Poland in 1764.
questions.answer: Stanislaw Poniatowski (accept Stanislaw II Augustus; prompt on Stanislaw)

questions.clue: Co-editor, with Diderot, of the Encyclop´edie; his work on wave equations was supported by Catherine.
Treaty that ended war with Turkey in 1792, signed in Moldavia.
Country it was part of until 1965, when it was expelled.
First Prime Minister of independent Singapore, who famously cried upon its expulsion.
Political party that has led Singapore since its independence.
Largest opposition party in Singapore; it shares its name with North Korea's ruling party
Punishment used in Singapore on American teenager Michael Fay in 1994 for vandalism.
questions.answer: caning (accept descriptions of being beaten with a cane; prompt on beating)

questions.clue: British explorer who is considered the founder of modern Singapore.
Famous landmark in Singapore's Bayfront area consisting of three towers connected by a rooftop SkyPark.
First Viceroy of India, who names an early British fort built on the grounds of the original governor's mansion.
questions.answer: Charles John Canning (accept Earl Canning or Viscont Canning; accept Fort Canning (Hill))

setName: 2019 HS Nationals History Bowl Round 7

questions.clue: State in which the battle took place.
National holiday that immediately followed the last day of fighting.
Supposed inventor of baseball, a Union officer who took over from the killed John Reynolds on the first day.
Union commander who replaced Joseph Hooker days before the battle.
Futile Confederate action on the last day that established the "High Water Mark of the Confederacy."
Name of the defensive position attacked in that futile action, at the center of the fish-hook line.
Peak south of town on the "barb" of the fish-hook line; Richard Ewell attacked it all three days.
Union officer who lost his leg at Gettysburg, four years after he murdered the son of Francis Scott Key and was acquitted via temporary insanity.
Region, a part of the United Kingdom, where it took place.
questions.answer: Northern Ireland (do not accept or prompt on Ireland alone)

questions.clue: Paramilitary organization that was the largest group to fight the British in the Troubles.
questions.answer: Provisional Irish Republican Army (or Provisional IRA)

questions.clue: April 1998 agreement that ended the Troubles.
Incident in 1972 in which British soldiers shot 28 civilians in Bogside.
Politician who led Sinn Fein [shin fayn] from 1983-2018 and was targeted for assassination during the Troubles.
Province that names a "Defence Association" that carried out that assassination attempt.
British overseas territory where 3 men were killed as part of Operation Flavius by the SAS.
British counter-insurgence unit, described as a legalized death squad, that set up a massage parlor as a front and was replaced by the SRU.
Was home to a bobsled team that gained worldwide fame after the Calgary Olympics.
Was the site of the Parsley Massacre, in which people fled one of its countries for the other.
Was taken by the Dutch in 1636 and joins Bonaire and Curacao [kur-ah-sow] in the ABC islands.
Saw its capital city destroyed by the Soufriere Hills volcano in 1995.
Saw both its French and Dutch sides devastated by Hurricane Irma in 2017.
Named a public square after its Nobel laureate poet, Derek Walcott.
Was home to a controversial US naval bombing range near Puerto Rico until 2003.
questions.answer: Vieques ([bee-eh-kays], but phonetic pronunciations are acceptable, as usual)

questions.clue: Seceded from St. Kitts and Nevis to become a separate crown colony in 1980.
